name: San Caprasio Circular Trail from Farlete
name_original: Cuevas de San Caprasio desde la Ermita de la Virgen de la Sabina — trail running circular por la Sierra de Alcubierre
description: This difficult 10.6-mile circular trail running route in the Sierra de Alcubierre offers panoramic views and historical caves.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Farlete offer diverse landscapes for runners, situated near Zaragoza in Aragon, Spain. The region is characterized by varied terrain, including Mediterranean forests and the Sierra de Alcubierre, providing a mix of challenging paths and scenic views. Runners can explore routes that traverse hills and distinctive natural features, making it a suitable area for different fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Farlete The most popular jogging route is San Caprasio Circular Trail from Farlete, a 10.6 miles (17.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 12 minutes to complete. This difficult route features an elevation gain of 384 meters. Another top favourite among local runners is Monegrillo Viewpoints Trail, a difficult 12.6 miles (20.3 km) path. This route offers expansive views and an elevation gain of 327 meters. Local runners also love the Running loop from Leciñena, a 11.5 miles (18.6 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 20 minutes. Jogging around Farlete is defined by Mediterranean forests, hills, and open landscapes, offering options for different ability levels. The area provides both moderate and difficult running routes. The routes in Farlete are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 20 runners have used komoot to explore Farlete's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in the Farlete area?
A: There are several dedicated jogging routes around Farlete, offering diverse experiences for runners. Our guide features 6 distinct routes, ranging from moderate to difficult, allowing you to explore the varied landscapes of the region.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging trails around Farlete?
A: The Farlete area, situated near Zaragoza in Aragon, Spain, offers a mix of terrains. You'll find trails traversing Mediterranean forests, hills, and open landscapes, particularly within the Sierra de Alcubierre. This variety provides both challenging paths and scenic views, catering to different running preferences.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running routes in Farlete?
A: While many routes in our guide are moderate to difficult, the region generally offers trails suitable for various fitness levels. You can find easier trails up to 3 miles with moderate elevation gain, often on mostly paved surfaces, which are great for beginners or a lighter run. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151701" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Nuez de Ebro&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option with less elevation.

Q: Can I find challenging trail running options near Farlete?
A: Absolutely. Farlete is known for its challenging trail running opportunities, with several routes exceeding 10 miles or featuring significant elevation gain. Trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983261477/san-caprasio-circular-trail-from-farlete" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Caprasio Circular Trail from Farlete&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983251798/monegrillo-viewpoints-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monegrillo Viewpoints Trail&lt;/a&gt; are classified as difficult, demanding good fitness and sure-footedness for their rugged terrain and impressive ascents.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Farlete region?
A: Yes, circular routes are a popular feature in the Farlete area. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983261477/san-caprasio-circular-trail-from-farlete" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Caprasio Circular Trail from Farlete&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering a loop that combines natural beauty with historical caves. Many other trails in the region are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I see while running in Farlete?
A: The running routes around Farlete offer a chance to explore both natural beauty and historical sites. You might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5268339" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Caprasio Caves&lt;/a&gt;, which are part of the San Caprasio Circular Trail, or pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300070" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of the Sabina&lt;/a&gt;. The region's Mediterranean forests and the Sierra de Alcubierre also provide stunning natural backdrops.

Q: Are the running trails around Farlete suitable for families?
A: While some routes are quite challenging, Farlete does offer options that can be enjoyed by families, especially those with some experience in outdoor activities. Look for shorter, less strenuous sections or trails described as 'easy' in other guides. Always check the route's distance and elevation profile to ensure it matches your family's fitness level.

Q: What is the best time of year for running in Farlete?
A: The best time for running in Farlete is generally during the spring and autumn months when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, especially in the Monegros desert terrain, making early mornings or late evenings preferable. Winters are usually mild, but checking local weather forecasts for rain or colder conditions is always advisable.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Farlete?
A: Many outdoor trails in the Farlete region are dog-friendly, but it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock. Be mindful of local regulations, and ensure your dog is well-behaved and doesn't disturb other runners or the natural environment. Always carry water for your pet, especially on longer runs.

Q: Where can I find panoramic views on the running routes?
A: For expansive views, consider routes that traverse higher elevations or pass through specific viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983251798/monegrillo-viewpoints-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monegrillo Viewpoints Trail&lt;/a&gt; is specifically designed to offer excellent vistas. Additionally, the Sierra de Alcubierre provides numerous spots with panoramic outlooks over the surrounding landscapes, including the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5312129" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de la Estiva&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5753138" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enebro Peak&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is there parking available near the trailheads in Farlete?
A: Yes, parking is generally available near the starting points of many running trails in and around Farlete. Specific parking information can often be found on individual route descriptions or local signage. It's advisable to check the details for your chosen route beforehand, especially for more remote trailheads.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Farlete?
A: The running routes in Farlete are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars from 2 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, the challenging nature of some paths, and the beautiful natural scenery, including the Mediterranean forests and historical caves. Many appreciate the opportunity to explore the unique landscapes of the Sierra de Alcubierre.

Q: Are there any running events or races in the Farlete area?
A: Farlete is a known hub for trail running and hosts events such as the 'Burraco Trail Farlete.' This event is part of a trail running league that utilizes the impressive landscapes of Farlete and neighboring towns like Perdiguera and Leciñena. These races are designed to challenge runners across various skill levels with demanding paths and distinctive scenery.
tips: Near the town of Farlete, we find the Hermitage of the Virgen de la Sabina, which is undoubtedly worth making a stop on the way and coming to see the interior of this sanctuary so well cared for and preserved by the group of residents of Farlete. One of the most beautiful Sanctuaries of Los Monegros

We are at the viewpoint that bears the name of the hermitage of San Caprasio. From here we will have spectacular views of the Ebro valley and other regions that we can distinguish on the information panel.

These caves, carved out of the rock, were restored in the 1950s by the Brotherhood of Jesus, which makes them available for walkers and spiritual retreats. Previously it is said that they were used by the bandit "Cucaracha" who frequented this area and hid in the cave where the hermitage is today.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Caves of San Caprasio trail?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking near the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Sabina, which serves as the starting point for this circular trail. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends or during the annual pilgrimage, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner trail runners?
faqs.answer: This trail is classified as 'difficult' for jogging on komoot, and 'moderate' for hiking. While it features easily-accessible paths, its 17 km distance and 384 meters of elevation gain mean it's best suited for experienced joggers with good fitness levels. Beginners might find it challenging and may need to walk some sections.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily follows easily-accessible paths within the Sierra de Alcubierre. You'll encounter varied landscapes, including sections with panoramic views of the Monegros region. Some parts may involve inclines, especially leading up to the peak of San Caprasio.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass several notable spots. The trail starts near the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Sabina. A major highlight is the San Caprasio Caves, which are rock-carved hermitages with historical significance. You'll also reach the San Caprasio Viewpoint, offering excellent panoramic views.

faqs.question: Is the Caves of San Caprasio trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, trails in the Sierra de Alcubierre are dog-friendly. However, as this area is part of a Natura 2000 site, it's crucial to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety, especially near livestock or sensitive natural areas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best times to run this trail. The weather is milder, making the 17 km distance and elevation gain more comfortable. In spring, you might also experience the area's natural beauty in full bloom. Summers can be very hot, and winters may bring colder temperatures and potentially challenging conditions.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this trail running route?
faqs.answer: For an experienced jogger, the route is estimated to take around 2 hours and 12 minutes to complete. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make at viewpoints or the caves, and current trail conditions.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this trail in Sierra de Alcubierre?
faqs.answer: No, you do not typically need permits or have to pay entrance fees to access this trail or the Sierra de Alcubierre. The area is open for public enjoyment, but always respect local regulations and leave no trace.

faqs.question: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is rich in history and culture. The San Caprasio Caves were historically hermitages and are associated with legends like the bandit 'Cucaracha.' An annual pilgrimage (romería) to the Hermitage of San Caprasio takes place every April 25th, highlighting the area's spiritual significance.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, parts of this circular route overlap with other local paths. Specifically, about 12% of the trail follows the 'Camino de San Caprasio' and a smaller section (2%) is part of the 'Senda San Caprasio'.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or services near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The trailhead is located near the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Sabina. While there might not be extensive commercial services directly at the trailhead, nearby towns like Alcubierre or Robres would offer amenities such as shops, cafes, and accommodation.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of the Virgin of the Sabina
